{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.
== External Links == * [https://sites.google.com/site/seishinkan302 Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan]
== Notes == {{reflist|2}}

== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.
== External Links == * [https://sites.google.com/site/seishinkan302 Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan]
== Notes == {{reflist|2}}

'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}

{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}

{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/qq/17/4076qq.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-qq-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. === Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/qq/17/4076qq.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-qq-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.
== External Links == * [https://sites.google.com/site/seishinkan302 Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan]

{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/qq/17/4076qq.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-qq-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.

He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/qq/17/4076qq.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-qq-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.
== External Links == * [https://sites.google.com/site/seishinkan302 Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan]

{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/qq/17/4076qq.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-qq-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.
== External Links == * [https://sites.google.com/site/seishinkan302 Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan]
== Notes == {{reflist|2}}

{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/qq/17/4076qq.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-qq-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.

'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/qq/17/4076qq.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-qq-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>

{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.
== External Links == * [https://sites.google.com/site/seishinkan302 Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan]
== Notes == {{reflist|2}}

'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.

{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.

{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.

'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>

{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.
== External Links == * [https://sites.google.com/site/seishinkan302 Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan]

'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>

Saeki passed away in 2005.

''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}

=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.

{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft|12=draft}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国|12=draft|12=draft}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.
== External Links == * [https://sites.google.com/site/seishinkan302 Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan]

revision.text:
{{Image requested|portrait|date=November 2024|cat=People}} {{Infobox academic | name = Arikiyo Saeki | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1925|3|2}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[Tokyo City]], [[Akasaka Ward]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2005|7|19|1925|3|2}} | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Japanese history]] (Ancient Japanese history) | alma_mater = [[Nagoya University]], [[University of Tokyo]] | workplaces = [[Hokkaido University]], [[Seijo University]] | degree = [[Doctor of Letters]] | URL = | yearsactive= | spouse = | children = }}
'''Arikiyo Saeki''' (佐伯 有清, March 2, 1925 – July 19, 2005) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Japanese history.
== Biography ==
; Early Life and Education Born in 1925 in [[Akasaka]], [[Tokyo City]], Saeki graduated from [[Hosei University Second High School]] and went on to study at the [[Nagoya University Faculty of Letters]] in the Department of Japanese History. He graduated in 1952 and completed a master's degree in Japanese history at the [[University of Tokyo Graduate School]] in 1957.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=佐伯 有清 |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/author/a13491.html |website=吉川弘文館 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
; Academic Career In 1952, Saeki began working at his alma mater, Hosei University Second High School, eventually becoming its principal.<ref name=":0" /> In 1962, he earned a Doctor of Letters from Nagoya University with his thesis on the *{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft|12=draft}}*. He was appointed professor at [[Hokkaido University]] in 1971 and later at [[Seijo University]] in 1983.<ref name=":0" /> He retired from Seijo University in 1995.
Saeki passed away in 2005.
== Awards and Honors == * 1984: Received the 74th [[Japan Academy Prize (Imperial)|Japan Academy Prize]] for *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (Research on the Shinsen Shōjiroku), published by [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]. * Spring 1995: Conferred the [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]], Third Class.
== Research and Contributions == Saeki’s research primarily focused on three areas: * Ancient [[Japanese history]] and studies on the {{ill|Yamatai Kingdom|ja|邪馬台国|12=draft|12=draft}}. * Early Japanese [[Buddhist thought]] history. * The formative period of the Japanese {{ill|Tendai sect|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}}.
=== Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan === After his death, his family donated his personal library, leading to the establishment of the Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan in [[Ayase City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Father's Memory: Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library Established |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0402/2017/11/17/407611.html |website=タウンニュース |date=2017-11-17 |access-date=2022-07-09}}</ref>
== Selected Publications == * *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū* (9 volumes), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1971–1984. ''' On-demand edition, 2007. {{colbegin|2}} # Text Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009157</ref> # Research Volume<ref>ISBN 9784642009164</ref> # Commentary Volume 1<ref>ISBN 9784642009171</ref> # Commentary Volume 2<ref>ISBN 9784642009188</ref> # Commentary Volume 3<ref>ISBN 9784642009195</ref> # Commentary Volume 4<ref>ISBN 9784642009201</ref> # Commentary Volume 5<ref>ISBN 9784642009218</ref> # Commentary Volume 6<ref>ISBN 9784642009225</ref> # Index and Essays<ref>ISBN 9784642009232</ref> {{colend}}
* *Shinsen Shōjiroku no Kenkyū: Shūi-hen*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2001.
=== Other Notable Works === {{colbegin|2}} * *Exploring the Mysteries of Ancient History*, Yomiuri Shimbun, 1973. * *Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1974. * *Postwar Yamatai Research History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *Genealogies of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Gakuseisha, 1975. * *The Road to Ancient History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1975. * *The Kwanggaeto Stele and the General Staff Headquarters*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1976. * *Profiles of Ancient Japanese History*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1977. * *Japanese Ancient Politics and Society*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1978. * *The Last Envoys to Tang China*, Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 1978. ''' Republished as Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 2007. * *Ancient East Asia and Japan*, Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho, 1979. * *Research History of the Kwanggaeto Stele*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1981. {{colend}}
=== Co-edited Works === * *Japanese Ancient History Essays*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980. * *Basic Anthology on Yamatai-koku* (3 volumes), Sōgensha, 1981–1982. * *Essays on Ancient Japanese Politics*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983.
=== Edited Translations === * *The Records of the Three Kingdoms: Commentary on Japanese References*, Iwanami Bunko, 1988.
== External Links == * [https://sites.google.com/site/seishinkan302 Saeki Arikiyo Memorial Library: Seishinkan]
== Notes == {{reflist|2}} <!--Saeki Ariyoshi-->
